annotations.text: the most likely diagnosis is **vertebral osteomyelitis secondary to *Staphylococcus aureus***, seeded from the chronic paronychia.
The second most likely diagnosis is **Ewing sarcoma**, given the age, the solitary osteolytic lesion with soft-tissue mass, and the neurologic symptoms.
The third consideration is **tuberculosis**, though less likely without systemic signs or pulmonary findings.

The chronicity of the paronychia raises the question of whether the patient may have an underlying immunodeficiency, such as chronic granulomatous disease (CGD), which predisposes to recurrent staphylococcal skin and soft-tissue infections and can lead to osteomyelitis.
The diagnostic procedure performed—likely a biopsy via posterior approach or percutaneous needle biopsy—will be the key to definitive diagnosis.

The possibility of a spinal epidural hematoma is excluded by the timeline and imaging findings—the hematoma would not be chronic, would not cause osteolysis, and would not have a solid soft-tissue component on CT.

The biopsy will be diagnostic: if granulomas with caseation are found, TB is confirmed; if small round blue cells with CD99 positivity, Ewing sarcoma; if neutrophils and bacteria, bacterial osteomyelitis.
The elevated ESR and chronic infection history make infectious osteomyelitis the leading hypothesis, but malignancy cannot be ruled out without histopathology.
This case underscores the importance of considering both infectious and neoplastic causes in young patients with unexplained vertebral lesions, especially in the setting of a chronic skin infection.
